items.why: Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).

items.correct: A verb in the subjunctive mood.
items.why: Students use infinitive after impersonal expressions. 'Il faut que tu PARTES' (subjunctive), not 'il faut que tu partir'. The que triggers subjunctive in the following clause.

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my brother'?
items.correct: Je m'entends bien avec mon frère.
items.misconception: Je m'entend bien avec mon frère.
items.random_wrong: Je entends bien avec mon frère.
items.why: It's Je m'entends bien avec mon frère.. “Je m'entend bien avec mon frère.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which form of 'you' should you use when addressing your French teacher?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: vous. “tu” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many forms does the French adjective 'beau' have (masc sing, masc plur, fem sing, fem plur, masc before vowel 'bel')?
items.why: The real figure is 5 forms. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
